sentences.grammarNote: 시내 (sinae) = downtown/city centre — literally 'inside the city'. The particle 에 marks the destination: 시내에 나가요 (go out to downtown). 나가다 = to go out — 나 (out) + 가다 (go), a directional compound.
Seoul's subway system (지하철) is one of the largest in the world and extremely well-connected. Line announcements are made in Korean, English, Chinese, and Japanese — very foreigner-friendly.

**저기요** (jeogiyo) is the standard way to get a stranger's attention in public — 'excuse me!'
**어디예요?** (eodiyeyo?) = 'where is it?' — 어디 (where) + 예요 (polite copula).
**이/가** after 경복궁 is the subject marker. Here it identifies Gyeongbokgung as the topic of the question.
Note the difference: **어디예요?** (where is it?) vs. **어디에 있어요?** (where does it exist?) — both are correct but the first is slightly more casual.

〜다가 (daga) expresses doing something and then switching to another action: 직진하다가 (while going straight, then...). It implies the first action is interrupted or followed by the second — useful for giving directions in Korean.

sentences.base_language: I want to go to the museum.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜고 싶어요 (go sipeoyo) = 'want to do': 가고 싶어요 (want to go), 먹고 싶어요 (want to eat). Attach 고 싶다 to any verb stem. 박물관 (bakmulgwan) = museum — 박물 (博物, broad things) + 관 (館, hall/building).

sentences.base_language: Let's have lunch at Gwangjang Market.
sentences.grammarNote: 에서 (eseo) marks the location where an action takes place: 시장에서 먹다 (eat at the market). Use 에 for destination and existence; use 에서 for where an action happens. 광장시장 is one of Seoul's oldest markets, famous for street food.

sentences.base_language: There's a convenience store on every corner.
sentences.grammarNote: 마다 (mada) = 'every / each', attached directly to a noun: 모퉁이마다 (every corner), 날마다 (every day), 사람마다 (every person). 편의점 (pyeonuijeom) = convenience store — 편의 (便宜, convenience) + 점 (店, shop). Korea has one of the highest convenience store densities in the world.

즐겁다 (jeulgeobda) = to be enjoyable/fun — irregular ㅂ verb: 즐거워요 (present), 즐거웠어요 (past). Compare 재미있다 (fun/interesting) and 신나다 (exciting/thrilling) — all describe positive experiences but with slightly different nuances. 즐겁다 emphasises a pleasant, gratifying feeling.
